method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
The Hunga volcano lies in the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc in the South Pacific, a volcanic arc produced by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[1] This subduction system extends for about 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga.[1] The volcano is largely submarine, rising more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extending about 20 km across.[1]

The summit of the volcano is occupied by a large submarine caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter.[1] High-resolution multibeam sonar surveys identified a 4 × 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the site of the caldera, consistent with collapse during an earlier explosive eruption.[2] The islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai formed the small emergent northern and western remnants of the caldera rim.[1][2] Early geological observations described the two islands as elongated and tangent to a circle centered on a rocky shoal about 3 km south of Hunga Tonga, the site of eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[2]

Rocks sampled on Hunga Tonga consist of alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli, and ash that dip gently away from the inferred caldera centre.[2] The eruptive products of the Hunga volcano are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic volcanic arcs.[1] The morphology and deposits led researchers to interpret Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai as remnants of a larger active submarine caldera volcano.[2]

During the 2014–2015 eruption, a tephra cone joined Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai into a single island known as Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[1] The new island had an area of just over 1 km² and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m before the 2022 eruption.[4] By November 2021, despite shoreline erosion and settling, the island remained broadly similar in form to its late-2015 configuration.[2] The 15 January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the joined island, leaving only small remnants of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above sea level.[1][4]

==Geography==
Before the 2022 eruption, the exposed island was uninhabited and lay in Tonga about 65 km north of Tongatapu, the country’s main island, and about 30 km south of the submarine volcano Fonuafoʻou.[5] The above-water land area covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m.[5] Its short-lived landscape attracted geomorphological study because satellite monitoring could document rapid erosion and landform change on newly created volcanic terrain.[2] Researchers also considered the island useful as an Earth analogue for interpreting similar-looking landforms observed on Mars.[2] After the January 2022 eruption, the remaining exposed geography was reduced to small fragments at the western and eastern edges of the former island.[5] Ash from the eruption was distributed across the Haʻapai, Tongatapu, and ʻEua island groups, with reported thicknesses of about 5–50 mm; on Tongatapu, ashfall was commonly about 20–30 mm thick.[5] The associated tsunami affected several inhabited islands in Tonga and also propagated across the Pacific to coastlines in South America, North America, and Northeast Asia.[5]

==Eruptions==
The best-documented eruptive episode occurred on 15 January 2022 at about 5:00 p.m. local time, when a violent submarine eruption produced massive explosions and sent gas and ash about 36 miles (58 km) into the atmosphere.[5][1] Satellite analyses measured a vertical plume more than 30 km high with overshooting tops above 55 km, making it a record-height volcanic plume in the satellite era.[2] The European Space Agency described the event as the largest eruption of the 21st century and the biggest recorded anywhere on Earth in about 30 years.[1] Estimates from surface-pressure observations put the initial explosion’s energy release at 10–28 exajoules, probably greater than the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo and possibly comparable to the 1883 eruption of Krakatoa.[2] The explosion generated atmospheric pressure waves and a sonic boom that travelled around the globe twice.[1]

The eruption-generated tsunami produced run-up in Tonga reported as high as 65 ft (20 m) above sea level, destroyed hundreds of structures, displaced more than 1,500 people and caused four deaths in Tonga.[5] Waves elsewhere in the Pacific caused two deaths and an oil spill in Peru, and damage was reported in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia and New Zealand.[5] Because the nearest functioning seismic station was nearly 500 miles (800 km) away, the United States Geological Survey’s National Earthquake Information Center and Volcano Disaster Assistance Program developed methods to identify earthquakes near the volcano in the days and weeks after the eruption.[5] Later seafloor surveys found fine sandy mud and deep ash ripples as far as 50 km from the volcano, and Tonga’s domestic internet cable was found buried beneath about 100 ft (30 m) of sediment and ash.[5]

Scientific assessments characterized the eruption as the largest underwater explosion ever recorded by modern instruments.[3] The eruption injected unusually large amounts of water vapour into the stratosphere, increasing global stratospheric water by about 10%, much of which was expected to remain there through 2025.[3] It also released a moderate amount of sulfur dioxide, which was rapidly converted into sulfate aerosols capable of reflecting sunlight back into space.[4] Subsequent assessments found short-term perturbations to Southern Hemisphere stratospheric ozone but minor overall effects on the Antarctic ozone hole and surface climate.[3] Model studies concluded that the eruption did not cause the record-high global temperatures of 2023–2024, with an estimated global surface cooling influence of about 0.05 K that was indistinguishable from natural climate variability.[3] A UCLA-led study similarly found that the eruption cooled the Southern Hemisphere by about 0.1 °C and produced a slight net cooling effect on the planet from 2022, despite the warming influence of persistent stratospheric water vapour.[4]

==Footnotes==
* The 15 January 2022 eruption has been described as the largest recorded volcanic eruption in more than three decades and among the largest volcanic events observed with modern geophysical instruments.[1][5]
* The volcano is an underwater edifice in the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc, with its summit about 150 metres below sea level before the 2022 eruption.[5]
* The eruption produced a plume reported to have reached up to 57 kilometres in altitude and dispersed tephra across an area at least 600 kilometres in diameter.[5]
* One proposed trigger for the highly explosive phase was a submarine landslide that removed overlying rock and allowed the volcano to erupt violently.[1]
* The eruption generated tsunamis that affected Tonga and were detected across the Pacific, including in Australia, Japan, and along the west coasts of North and South America.[1]
* Studies of the event have emphasized that its tsunami generation involved coupled atmospheric–oceanographic processes, including resonance effects and meteotsunami behaviour.[2]
* The eruption injected an unusually large amount of water vapour into the stratosphere, producing different climatic effects from many land-based eruptions dominated by sulfate aerosol cooling.[3]
* Satellite-based analyses found that the mid-stratosphere cooled by about 4 K for more than a year, while the lower stratosphere warmed by about 1–2 K.[3]
* Later modelling and satellite analyses indicated that the eruption caused a net cooling of about 0.1 °C over the Southern Hemisphere by the end of 2022 and 2023, rather than a net warming.[4]
* The eruption expelled an estimated 2.9 × 10^15 grams of basaltic-andesitic volcanogenic material, including ash and pumice, into the South Pacific Gyre.[5]
* Satellite observations after ash deposition showed elevated surface-water chlorophyll-a, indicating a biological response in surrounding ocean waters.[5]

The Hunga volcano lies in the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc in the South Pacific, a volcanic arc produced by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[1] This subduction system extends for about 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga.[1] The volcano is largely submarine, rising more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extending about 20 km across.[1]

The summit of the volcano is occupied by a large submarine caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter.[1] High-resolution multibeam sonar surveys identified a 4 × 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the site of the caldera, consistent with collapse during an earlier explosive eruption.[2] The islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai formed the small emergent northern and western remnants of the caldera rim.[1][2] Early geological observations described the two islands as elongated and tangent to a circle centered on a rocky shoal about 3 km south of Hunga Tonga, the site of eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[2]

* The 15 January 2022 eruption has been described as the largest recorded volcanic eruption in more than three decades and among the largest volcanic events observed with modern geophysical instruments.[1][5]
* The volcano is an underwater edifice in the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc, with its summit about 150 metres below sea level before the 2022 eruption.[5]

* The eruption generated tsunamis that affected Tonga and were detected across the Pacific, including in Australia, Japan, and along the west coasts of North and South America.[1]
* Studies of the event have emphasized that its tsunami generation involved coupled atmospheric–oceanographic processes, including resonance effects and meteotsunami behaviour.[2]
* The eruption injected an unusually large amount of water vapour into the stratosphere, producing different climatic effects from many land-based eruptions dominated by sulfate aerosol cooling.[3]
* Satellite-based analyses found that the mid-stratosphere cooled by about 4 K for more than a year, while the lower stratosphere warmed by about 1–2 K.[3]
* Later modelling and satellite analyses indicated that the eruption caused a net cooling of about 0.1 °C over the Southern Hemisphere by the end of 2022 and 2023, rather than a net warming.[4]

* Satellite observations after ash deposition showed elevated surface-water chlorophyll-a, indicating a biological response in surrounding ocean waters.[5]
